data.title: Amgen, AstraZeneca gets FDA nod for Tezspire
data.description:
• Amgen (NASDAQ:AMGN) and AstraZeneca (NASDAQ:AZN) said that the FDA has approved Tezspire as a maintenance treatment for chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps in patients aged 12 and older.
  • This FDA approval is supported by data demonstrating the drug's efficacy and safety from the WAYPOINT Phase III clinical trial.
  • Additionally, applications for regulatory approval are currently being evaluated in Europe, China, Japan, and various other nations.

data.title: Regions Financial expects full-year 2025 net interest income growth of 3%–4% while advancing technology modernization
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Regions Financial Corporation (RF) Q3 2025

Management View

  • John Turner, President, CEO & Chairman, opened the call by highlighting strong quarterly earnings of $548 million, resulting in earnings per share of $0.61. On an adjusted basis, earnings were $561 million or $0.63 per share. Turner stated, "We delivered adjusted pretax pre-provision income of $830 million, a 4% increase year-over-year, and we generated a strong return on tangible common equity of 19%." He noted continued top-quartile deposit growth, above-peer median change in market share, and the lowest deposit cost among peers. The company grew total average deposits and accounts across consumer checking, small business, and wealth management. Loan commitments increased by approximately $2 billion year-to-date, and pipelines are almost doubled year-over-year. Turner emphasized ongoing portfolio shaping in higher-risk leveraged lending segments and a meaningful increase in loans refinanced off-balance sheet through debt capital markets. Turner also detailed progress on core technology modernization, with a commercial loan system upgrade to a cloud platform in the first half of 2026 and pilots for a cloud-based deposit system beginning late 2026, with full conversion anticipated in 2027.

  • David Turner, Senior EVP & CFO, reported, "Average loans grew 1%, while ending declined 1%." He noted continued portfolio shaping, with $900 million in targeted loans exited year-to-date and another $300 million to address by year-end. Turner noted, "Deposits remained strong overall," and said, "We now expect average deposits to be up low single digits versus the prior year." Regarding net interest income, he stated, "Net interest income was relatively stable linked quarter... net interest income grew modestly, benefiting primarily from new fixed-rate asset originations and reinvestments in today's elevated rate environment." On noninterest income, Turner said, "We see continued opportunity to grow clients through both new relationships and within our existing customer base."

Outlook

  • The company now expects full-year 2025 average loans to remain relatively stable versus 2024. Management increased expectations for full-year average deposit balances, now expecting average deposits to be up low single digits versus the prior year. Net interest margin is expected to rebound into the mid-3.60%s in the fourth quarter. David Turner said, "We now expect full-year 2025 net interest income to grow between 3% and 4%." Adjusted noninterest income is projected to grow between 4% and 5% versus 2024, and adjusted noninterest expense is expected to be up approximately 2% for the full year.

Financial Results

  • The quarter saw a strong performance in noninterest revenue, with wealth management generating another quarter of record fee income and capital markets, excluding CVA, reaching a record high. Adjusted noninterest income increased 6% linked quarter. Service charges rose 6%, and capital markets income, excluding CVA, increased 22% compared to the prior quarter. Adjusted noninterest expense increased 4% compared to the prior quarter, with salaries and benefits up 2%. Annualized net charge-offs as a percentage of average loans increased 8 basis points to 55 basis points. The allowance for credit losses decreased $30 million, and the ratio was reduced 2 basis points to 1.78%. The company ended the quarter with an estimated common equity Tier 1 ratio of 10.8%; adjusted to include AOCI, it increased from 9.3% to 9.5% quarter-over-quarter.

Q&A

  • Kenneth Usdin, Bernstein Autonomous LLP: Asked whether the charge-offs and portfolio shaping were aligned with previously identified portfolios of interest and for an update on other portfolios being watched. John Turner responded that office and transportation portfolios have been under scrutiny for some time, with most charge-offs related to office. He highlighted improvements: "We had over $900 million in reductions, almost $1 billion in reductions in classified loans during the quarter. Some portion of that was a result of upgrades."
  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler: Inquired about the rationale behind portfolio shaping actions. John Turner said it's driven by both credit risk appetite and returns, emphasizing, "we're aligning our credit risk appetite with expected returns in portfolios." David Turner added that the year's big change was in the leverage lending portfolio.
  • Steven Alexopoulos, TD Cowen: Sought clarity on margin guidance and deposit betas. David Turner explained, "The beauty of our hedging portfolio is to protect us as rates come down... we've been able to have a pretty resilient net interest margin regardless of what rate environment that we have."
  • John Pancari, Evercore: Asked whether current portfolio reductions could continue to increase. John Turner stated, "We don't anticipate any significant additional reductions," but noted ongoing reviews and capital allocation discipline.
  • Dave Rochester, Cantor Fitzgerald: Asked about the possibility for loan growth to outpace GDP next year. David Turner reiterated expectation to grow in line with GDP plus a little, pending guidance.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst questions reflected a neutral to slightly positive tone, with some focus on credit risk, loan growth prospects, and the sustainability of positive operating leverage.
  • Management maintained a confident tone throughout, emphasizing strong credit quality, soundness of strategic plans, and capital allocation discipline. During Q&A, responses remained measured and consistent, with John Turner stating, "we feel really good about our NDFI exposure" and David Turner consistently referencing confidence in guidance and cost controls. Compared to the previous quarter, management sentiment remained steady, with a slightly greater emphasis on credit discipline and risk management.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company reported higher earnings and EPS compared to the previous quarter, with a continued strong return on tangible common equity. Guidance for net interest income growth narrowed slightly to between 3% and 4% from the prior 3%–5% range. Credit discipline and portfolio shaping remained a focus, with targeted loan exits and improvements in criticized loans. The tone of management and analysts remained similar, with management reiterating confidence in strategy and analysts maintaining a focus on loan growth, margin sustainability, and credit quality. Strategic priorities continue to emphasize technology modernization, risk-adjusted returns, and deposit growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified ongoing headwinds from higher-risk leveraged lending and continued elevated losses in certain portfolios through the fourth quarter. John Turner noted, "The only other area that we're seeing maybe a little elevated risk is in telecommunications." The company expects full-year net charge-offs to be approximately 50 basis points and has reserved for remaining anticipated losses. Analysts questioned whether further portfolio shaping could present future headwinds, to which management reiterated ongoing review but did not anticipate significant additional reductions.

Final Takeaway

Regions Financial Corporation emphasized strong earnings growth, resilient deposit and loan performance, and ongoing portfolio shaping focused on credit risk and returns. Management highlighted robust noninterest income, record results in wealth management and capital markets, and continued investment in technology modernization. The outlook for 2025 includes net interest income growth of 3%–4% and stable-to-modestly growing loan and deposit balances, with a consistent focus on prudent capital allocation and maintaining flexibility to adapt to evolving market and regulatory conditions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Community West Bancshares GAAP EPS of $0.57 beats by $0.03
data.description:
• Community West Bancshares press release (NASDAQ:CWBC): Q3 GAAP EPS of $0.57 beats by $0.03.

  • The Company declared a $0.12 per common share cash dividend, payable on November 14, 2025 to shareholders of record as of October 31, 2025.

data.title: Ferrellgas announces pricing of $650M senior notes offering
data.description:
• Ferrellgas (OTCPK:FGPR) and its wholly-owned subsidiary Ferrellgas Finance have priced an offering of $650M aggregate principal amount of 9.250% senior notes due 2031 at an offering price equal to 100% of the principal thereof.
  • The offering of the notes is expected to close on or about October 27, 2025.
  • The issuers intend to use the net proceeds received from the offering of the notes, together with cash on hand, to redeem all of their 5.375% senior notes due 2026. The redemption of the 2026 notes is conditioned upon the completion of the proposed offering of notes and the entry into an amendment to the credit agreement governing the Company’s existing revolving credit facility.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Wall Street overcomes worries on regional banks' health, government shutdown carries on
data.description:
U.S. stocks wavered amid increased volatility on Friday but managed to resurge, as Wall Street was rattled by concerns on the health of regional banks and credit risks while the U.S. government shutdown entered its 17th day.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) ended +0.5%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) closed +0.5%, and the Dow (DJI) also finished +0.5%.

Week-to-date, the S&P 500 rose 1.7%, while the Nasdaq advanced 2.1%, and the Dow was 1.6% higher.

“The equity indices were a nothingburger this week, troubling neither the highs nor the lows of last week. An option seller’s dream, given that the VIX spiked today to DeepSeek Crisis levels and for at least twelve hours the fear of a major credit default haunted the minds of investors,” Seeking Alpha analyst Alex King of Cestrian Capital Research said.

CBOE Volatility Index reached a six-month high of 28.99 on Friday, last 20.8.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 4 basis points to 4.01%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 4 basis points to 3.47%.

“Of more interest, bonds started to break to the upside, crypto remained subdued, and gold – yes – dropped a little! We look forward to the traditional Q4 rally in stocks,” King added.

Stocks of investment bank Jefferies Financial (JEF) +5.8%, regional banks Zions Bancorporation (ZION) +5.8%, and Western Alliance (WAL) +3.3% were initially lower on Friday as investors worried about the quality of loans and investments made by the firms, but since recovered, all closing higher.

Goldman Sachs (GS) and J.P. Morgan (JPM), however, were some of the top decliners in the Dow (DJI), ending -1%, and -0.2%, respectively.

The KBW Nasdaq Regional Banking Index (KRX) closed down 6.3% on Thursday trading, its biggest drop since April, following the disclosures by two banks of irregularities related to some loans -- ending +1.7%.

On trade, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent is expected to speak by phone later on Friday with Chinese Vice Premier He Lifeng about the ongoing trade negotiations, according to the Trump administration.

Meanwhile, the U.S. government shutdown continued to grind on as it entered its third week, after a GOP funding bill failed for the 10th time. Prediction market Kalshi now sees the government shutdown lasting more than 41 days, the longest ever.

On the economic side, Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis President Alberto Musalem said the Fed could consider another rate cut if the labor market continues to weaken.

Also, federal worker jobless claims surged to the highest level since the last government shutdown in 2019 at 7,224 initial claims.

Wall Street's major averages closed lower on Thursday amid increased volatility due to the ongoing U.S.-China trade conflict and bad loan reports in banks, while investors continued to assess earnings reports and the ongoing U.S. government shutdown.

data.title: GIBO Holdings announces transfer to Nasdaq Capital Market
data.description:
• GIBO Holdings (NASDAQ:GIBO) on Friday said that it has received approval from the Nasdaq Listing Qualifications department of The Nasdaq Stock Market LLC to transfer the listing of the company's Class A ordinary shares from the Nasdaq Global Market to the Nasdaq Capital Market.
  • The shares will be transferred to the Nasdaq Capital Market effective as of the open of business on October 20, 2025, and will continue to trade under the symbol "GIBO."
  • Meanwhile, the company's warrants to purchase Class A ordinary shares will remain listed on the Nasdaq Capital Market under the symbol "GIBOW."
  • GIBO -1.37% after hours to $2.515.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Federal worker jobless claims jump to highest level since 2019 shutdown
data.description:
Jobless claims from U.S. federal workers surged to their highest mark since the 2019 government shutdown, according to data from state unemployment insurance program offices released Friday.

For the week ended Oct. 11, there were 7,224 initial claims filed in the Unemployment Compensation for Federal Employees program, more than doubling from the prior week and the highest since early 2019.

During a government shutdown, the Labor Department pauses its weekly jobless claims reports, though states and territories can continue submitting their data.

Several states were not included in the latest week of data, including Arizona, Massachusetts, Nevada, Tennessee or the Virgin Islands.

It was recently reported that the Trump administration has sent layoff notices to thousands of federal employees across multiple agencies, amid the shutdown, which is now stretching into its 17th day.

Federal staffers placed on furlough during a shutdown may qualify for unemployment benefits, depending on state rules. In most cases, those benefits must be repaid once back pay is issued, according to a fact sheet.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.
